title: Black bun
subtitle: Scottish Hogmanay spiced fruit loaf in a pastry case
excerpt: Black bun is a densely spiced fruit mixture encased in a thin shortcrust pastry shell. It is a Scottish Hogmanay tradition, baked weeks in advance and carried by first-footers on New Year's night. The filling is almost entirely fruit and spice; the pastry exists to hold it together.
sourceNotes: Black bun is a documented Scottish Hogmanay tradition with roots at least as far back as the seventeenth century, when it was associated with Twelfth Night before the Reformation shifted the celebration to New Year. It appears in Scottish cookery records through the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries and is still commercially produced today. The recipe here follows the standard Scottish home-baking tradition: a spiced fruit filling of currants, raisins, and mixed peel in a plain shortcrust case.

recipe.freezeNotes: Cool completely. Wrap tightly and freeze for up to 2 months. Thaw slowly in the fridge overnight.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Black bun improves with age. Make it at least 2 days ahead and keep tightly wrapped at room temperature. It keeps well for up to 2 weeks. Some recipes mature it for a month.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; the bun needs the full 85 to 90 minutes for the centre of the fruit filling to reach temperature

glossaryTerms.definition: The pastry casing for black bun is not meant to be eaten on its own in the way a pie crust is. It is a structural shell that holds the dense, moist fruit filling during baking and maturing. Use a shortcrust ratio of flour to fat (2:1 by weight) made with lard or butter. Roll it thin, around 4 mm, and press it firmly into the tin with no gaps. Reserve enough to make the lid.

content.text: Black bun tastes primarily of spice and dried fruit with almost no sweetness from the pastry itself. The
is structural rather than decorative. Make the pastry first, line the tin, and fill it cold from the fridge. The filling is assembled dry.

items.prepNote: beaten, for the filling and to glaze

content.text: Rub 60 g cold lard and 60 g cold butter into 250 g plain flour with 3 g salt until the mixture resembles coarse breadcrumbs. Add 3 to 4 tbsp cold water and mix to a firm dough. Wrap and refrigerate for 30 minutes. Roll out two-thirds of the pastry on a floured surface to around 4 mm thickness. Line a greased 2 lb loaf tin, pressing the pastry firmly into the corners and up the sides with enough overhang to crimp the lid onto. Refrigerate while you make the filling.

Heat the oven to 180 degrees C fan. Mix together 300 g currants, 200 g raisins, 75 g mixed peel, optional 75 g chopped almonds, 50 g plain flour, 100 g dark brown sugar, 1 tsp ginger, 1 tsp mixed spice, 1 tsp cinnamon, and 0.5 tsp bicarbonate of soda. Add 1 tbsp black treacle, half the beaten egg, and 60 ml milk. Mix until combined. The filling should be moist but not wet. Pack firmly into the chilled pastry-lined tin.

Roll the remaining pastry into a lid to fit the tin. Press it onto the filling and crimp the edges firmly to the sides. Brush with the remaining beaten egg. Use a skewer to prick the lid all over in a pattern, then push the skewer straight down through the filling to the base at four points to create steam vents. Bake for 85 to 90 minutes until the pastry is deep golden. Cool completely in the tin before turning out. Wrap tightly and keep for at least 2 days before cutting.

items.problem: Pastry cracks and the filling bursts through
items.solution: The pastry was rolled too thin, or there were gaps at the crimping point. Roll to 4 mm minimum and crimp firmly. The steam vents help prevent pressure build-up.

items.problem: Centre of the filling is still wet when sliced
items.solution: The bun was underbaked. Bake the full 90 minutes and check that the pastry is uniformly golden. A skewer pushed into the filling through the vent hole should come out with only dry crumbs after 90 minutes.
